extraction_rationale: Named carbon farming/sequestration pilot strategy with quantified supporting actions.
mechanism_description: Conducts pilot projects and research on carbon-sequestration farming practices (including biosolids application) to increase soil carbon storage and improve ecosystem condition.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem enhancement (regenerative/agroecological land management) → ↑ soil carbon + improved ecosystem function → ↓ net atmospheric GHG forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changed activity/system: Farming and food-system practices shift to regenerative/agroecological management (e.g., cover crops, rotations, compost/manure incorporation, reduced tillage, improved grazing, agroforestry/afforestation with soil-improving species), often complemented by plant-based food promotion and food-waste minimization.  
(2) Mediating process: Increased organic matter inputs and reduced soil disturbance enhance soil organic carbon formation and retention; improved vegetation cover and biodiversity reduce erosion and improve nutrient/water cycling; in some cases reduced enteric/livestock and landfill-related emissions via diet/waste changes.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net GHG mitigation primarily via carbon sequestration in soils (and sometimes biomass) and secondarily via reduced CH₄/N₂O/CO₂ emissions from food production and waste.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increasing net carbon storage (soil organic carbon and sometimes associated biomass) and thereby reducing net atmospheric GHG concentrations.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core causal lever is land/ecosystem management that increases soil carbon and restores ecosystem function, yielding net carbon removal/sequestration (with additional avoided emissions possible).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “Mitigation” is also true, the dominant mechanism operates through ecosystem enhancement/carbon sinks (a hallmark of Nature-Based Solutions) rather than primarily through energy efficiency or purely technological abatement. “Resource Efficiency” (food waste reduction) and demand-side mitigation (plant-based diets) can be important but are not the defining pathway for all policies in this mechanism set.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some policies under this umbrella may emphasize dietary shift or food-waste reduction more than soil carbon; if the mechanism is truly driven by demand-side emissions reduction rather than sequestration, that would shift classification toward Mitigation/Resource Efficiency at the policy level—but that would be a different canonical mechanism than “soil_carbon_and_ecosystem_restoration.”  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose canonical mechanism is regenerative agriculture → soil carbon & ecosystem restoration necessarily relies on ecosystem processes (soil carbon accumulation/ecosystem recovery) as the primary climate lever, matching Nature-Based Solutions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., soil carbon sequestration vs. agroforestry vs. food-waste co-measure) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Policies that primarily promote plant-based diets or food-waste reduction without a clear regenerative/soil-carbon/ecosystem-restoration land-management component would not fit this mechanism and could be primarily Mitigation or Resource Efficiency; mixed packages may warrant separating mechanisms when coding at policy level.
key_indicators: “Conduct carbon sequestration farming pilot projects and research”; “Supporting Actions”; “apply approximately 500 wet tons of biosolids per year”; “expand its carbon mitigation and offset program”
co_benefits: Soil health and fertility improvement; waste diversion/beneficial use of biosolids; water retention and erosion reduction; potential biodiversity benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could arguably be coded as an incentive-program if the pilots are implemented via grants/payments to farms, but the provided text emphasizes pilots/research (program/planning) rather than explicit financial incentives or mandates.

extraction_rationale: Quantified annual application commitment with deadline and purpose.
mechanism_description: The policy plans to apply biosolids as a soil amendment on identified sites (e.g., mine reclamation projects) to increase carbon stored in soils.
primary_causal_pathway: Organic amendment application → ↑ soil organic matter formation/stabilization → ↑ soil carbon stocks (carbon sink) → ↓ net atmospheric CO₂
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Land managers apply treated wastewater biosolids to soils (e.g., reclaimed mine lands, degraded sites) as an organic soil amendment, often coupled with site preparation and vegetation establishment.  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Added organic inputs are incorporated into soil and partially stabilized via aggregation and mineral association; improved soil conditions can increase plant growth and belowground carbon inputs, further building soil organic carbon.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net increase in terrestrial carbon sequestration (higher soil carbon stocks) reduces net GHG concentrations, subject to managing potential countervailing emissions (e.g., N₂O from added nitrogen, CH₄ if applied under anaerobic conditions).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is increasing net carbon stored in soils by adding and stabilizing organic carbon from biosolids, thereby removing CO₂ from the atmosphere over time.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s core pathway is enhancement of a biological/soil carbon sink through organic amendment application leading to increased soil carbon storage.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although biosolids reuse can have Resource Efficiency elements (diverting organic residuals from disposal) and can affect Mitigation via avoided landfill emissions, the stated and mechanism-defining effect here is *soil carbon sequestration* (sink enhancement), which is most directly Nature-Based Solutions.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy’s primary intent/effect were disposal diversion (avoided landfill CH₄) or fertilizer displacement (avoided industrial N production), the primary category could shift; however, those are not intrinsic to this canonical mechanism definition focused on soil carbon sequestration.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that applies biosolids specifically to increase soil carbon stocks relies on ecosystem/soil processes to store additional carbon, fitting Nature-Based Solutions across contexts (reclamation, rangeland, forestry, degraded soils).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Co-benefits/risks (e.g., avoided disposal emissions, fertilizer substitution, N₂O tradeoffs, water retention/adaptation benefits) require policy-specific evidence and are deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential countervailing emissions (notably N₂O from nitrogen additions, CH₄ under waterlogged conditions) can reduce net climate benefit; if these dominate in a given implementation, the net effect may be smaller. Some programs may frame biosolids land application primarily as waste diversion or nutrient recycling; those should be classified by their dominant quantified pathway, but this canonical mechanism is defined by soil carbon sequestration.
key_indicators: “By 2024”; “apply approximately 500 wet tons of biosolids per year”; “as a soil amendment”; “to sequester carbon”; “newly identified sites such as mine reclamation projects in Northern California”
co_benefits: Soil rehabilitation/improved soil fertility and structure; mine land reclamation and erosion control; potential waste diversion/beneficial reuse of biosolids
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be implemented via procurement/contracts or a program requirement, but the explicit quantified goal and deadline make “target-setting” the best fit based on the text alone.

primary_causal_pathway: Organic waste management change → ↓ landfilled organics → ↓ anaerobic decay in landfills → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ net GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Cities expand source-separated organics collection, local composting capacity, and end-markets/use set for compost (e.g., city projects, agriculture).  
(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced anaerobic decomposition of biodegradable waste in landfills lowers methane generation; composting favors aerobic decomposition (primarily CO₂ rather than CH₄) and stabilizes organic matter; compost application returns nutrients/organic matter to soils (potentially improving soil carbon and reducing synthetic fertilizer demand).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in GHG emissions dominated by avoided CH₄ from landfills, with potential secondary benefits from increased soil carbon storage and reduced upstream fertilizer-related emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoiding landfill methane emissions by diverting organics to composting (and associated organics management) instead of landfilling.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: divert organics from landfill → avoid methane → reduce net GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while nutrient recovery/soil improvement can contribute to resource efficiency and sometimes nature-based outcomes, the largest and most consistent climate impact of this mechanism is CH₄ avoidance from landfill diversion, which is directly mitigation-focused.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a program’s compost use is explicitly designed mainly for soil carbon sequestration or heat/drought resilience, those could support Nature-Based Solutions/Adaptation as co-benefits; however, within this canonical mechanism the landfill methane avoidance remains the defining dominant pathway.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: any policy that expands composting/diverts organics from landfill inherently reduces methane generation relative to landfilling, making mitigation the common primary category across contexts (Austin, Brussels, Chicago).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether secondary labels (e.g., soil carbon, fertilizer displacement, circularity/resource efficiency, resilience co-benefits) apply depends on policy-specific evidence (feedstock, processing method, landfill gas capture baseline, compost application practices), and should be assigned at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “composting” pathway is actually anaerobic digestion with biogas capture/use, the dominant mitigation may shift toward renewable energy substitution in addition to (or instead of) landfill CH₄ avoidance; similarly, where landfills already have high-efficiency gas capture, incremental CH₄ avoidance may be smaller and compost-use soil carbon benefits could become relatively more important, but the canonical mechanism still centers on landfill diversion/methane reduction.

co_benefits: Reduced landfill volume; improved soil health/fertility; potential reductions in synthetic fertilizer use; local waste system efficiency improvements

section_header: THE PATH TO REACH NET-ZERO BY 2040 / EMISSIONS TARGETS AND CLIMATE GOALS
policy_statement: Reduce San Francisco sector-based greenhouse gas emissions by at least 61% below 1990 levels by 2030 and achieve net-zero sector-based emissions by 2040 (at least a 90% reduction below 1990), sequestering any residual emissions through nature-based solutions.
verbatim_text:
By integrating these four climate justice commitments, the CAP proposes two ambitious and achievable climate emission reduction targets:
- An interim target of cutting sector-based emissions 61% below 1990 levels by 2030; and
- Net-zero sector-based emissions by 2040, a 90% reduction from 1990 levels

TABLE 5: 2021 CLIMATE ACTION TARGETS
By 2040, achieve net-zero emissions by reducing emissions at least 90%compared to 1990 levels and sequester any residual emissions through nature- based solutions
extraction_rationale: Citywide quantified emissions targets with deadlines and defined net-zero mechanism (90% reduction plus sequestration).
mechanism_description: The CAP proposes interim and net-zero sector-based emissions targets and plans to sequester any remaining emissions through nature-based solutions, driving an overall pathway to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance → cross-sector decarbonization actions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts a binding or guiding community-wide emissions target (e.g., % reduction by a year, net-zero), aligning planning, budgeting, regulations, and investments across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels, improved energy efficiency, fuel switching/electrification, methane reductions, and other sector measures lower the mass flow of GHGs released to the atmosphere.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower community-wide GHG emissions (CO₂e) leading to reduced cumulative atmospheric GHG concentrations and reduced radiative forcing over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is achieving economy-wide reductions in greenhouse gas emissions (often to net-zero) across major sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central function is to set an economy-wide emissions outcome that triggers and coordinates decarbonization measures, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly improve efficiency or resilience, their defining and largest intended climate effect is emissions mitigation at the community scale, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not resource efficiency as an end in itself, and not ecosystem-based carbon sequestration as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets include “net-zero” via offsets or land-sector sinks; even then, the dominant climate-relevant purpose remains achieving net emissions reductions/neutrality (mitigation). If a document is purely aspirational with no implementation linkage, it is still categorized by intended dominant pathway (mitigation).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a community-wide GHG reduction or net-zero target shares the same primary mechanism—economy-wide emissions reduction—regardless of sector mix or local context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether delivery relies more on efficiency, electrification, renewables, waste methane controls, offsets, or nature-based sinks requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets framed mainly as “climate neutrality” can be met with offsets or external credits; classification remains Mitigation, but downstream interpretation should note whether reductions are in-boundary vs. compensated. Targets limited to municipal operations (not community-wide) would be a different mechanism and should not be conflated.

co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes an element of carbon sequestration (“nature-based solutions”) but the policy instrument itself is primarily an emissions target rather than an implementation instrument (e.g., procurement/incentives/regulation) for sequestration.

section_header: THE PATH TO REACH NET-ZERO BY 2040 / EMISSIONS TARGETS AND CLIMATE GOALS
policy_statement: Reduce consumption-based emissions by 40% below 1990 levels by 2030 and by 80% below 1990 levels by 2050 (expressed as less than 30 mtCO2e per household by 2030 and less than 10 mtCO2e per household by 2050).
verbatim_text:
San Francisco is determined to reduce the impacts of these outsourced emissions and has set two targets:
- A 40% reduction in consumption-based emissions by 2030
- An 80% reduction in consumption-based emissions by 2050

TABLE 5: 2021 CLIMATE ACTION TARGETS
By 2030, reduce consumption-based emissions to less than 30 mtCO2e per household, equivalent to a 40% reduction compared to 1990 levels
By 2050, reduce consumption-based emissions to less than 10 mtCO2e per household, equivalent to an 80% reduction compared to 1990 levels
extraction_rationale: Quantified consumption-based emissions reduction targets with deadlines and per-household metrics.
mechanism_description: The city has set targets to reduce consumption-based (outsourced) emissions over time, guiding actions that lower supply-chain and household-associated greenhouse gas emissions.
typology_evidence_quote: Reduce consumption-based emissions by 40% below 1990 levels by 2030 and by 80% below 1990 levels by 2050
primary_causal_pathway: Consumption patterns + procurement set → ↓ demand for high-embodied-carbon goods/services → ↓ supply-chain (Scope 3) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city sets and uses a consumption-based emissions target to steer resident behavior, business practices, and institutional/public procurement toward lower-carbon products, services, diets, materials, and waste outcomes.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive production and logistics leads suppliers to shift to lower-carbon energy/material inputs and processes (and/or reduces overall production), cutting CO₂, CH₄, and N₂O across upstream manufacturing, transport, and end-of-life treatment.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in global GHG emissions attributable to the city’s consumption footprint, lowering cumulative emissions and climate forcing.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced global lifecycle (Scope 3/consumption-based) GHG emissions from goods and services consumed in the city.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: A consumption-based target primarily functions by driving reductions in lifecycle/supply-chain GHG emissions associated with local consumption, yielding net global emissions reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While many actions under the target may improve efficiency (resource efficiency) or reduce waste, those are instrumental levers; the defining intended outcome is GHG reduction (mitigation). It is not primarily about reducing hazard exposure (adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: Some implementations could focus heavily on waste/material efficiency (appearing “resource efficiency”), but the canonical mechanism is explicitly a GHG-reduction target for consumption-based emissions, so mitigation remains dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy framed as a consumption-based/outsourced emissions reduction target has the same primary climate objective—lowering attributable Scope 3 emissions—regardless of which sectors/actions are used to achieve it.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., circular economy, sustainable procurement, behavior change, waste prevention) depend on the actual measures used to meet the target and must be assigned at the individual policy level with supporting evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential ambiguity arises if a “consumption-based target” is used mainly as an accounting/monitoring exercise without enforceable measures; however, the mechanism as stated implies it guides actions to reduce embodied emissions, keeping the dominant effect mitigation.
key_indicators: “has set two targets”; “40% reduction in consumption-based emissions by 2030”; “80% reduction… by 2050”; “TABLE 5: 2021 CLIMATE ACTION TARGETS”; “less than 30 mtCO2e per household”; “less than 10 mtCO2e per household”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health benefits via reduced production/transport emissions; potential waste reduction/material efficiency benefits (not specified in the excerpt).
instance_edge_case_notes: Targets are consumption-based and household-normalized, but the text does not specify binding enforcement mechanisms or the specific policy levers (e.g., procurement rules, bans, incentives) that would implement the targets—so classification remains target-setting/enabling rather than regulatory or investment.

(2) Mediating physical process: Increased renewable electricity supply (via PPAs, RECs, utility green tariffs, or on-/off-site projects) reduces the need for fossil-fueled power plants to generate electricity, lowering combustion-related emissions (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in CO₂e emissions from electricity generation attributable to municipal electricity consumption, contributing to overall grid decarbonization and lower cumulative emissions.

3) Edge cases applied: If a policy only claims “renewable” via unbundled certificates with weak additionality, real-world emissions impacts can be smaller; however, the mechanism as stated still targets grid decarbonization via renewable sourcing and is best categorized as Mitigation.

BUILDINGS  By 2021, requiring zero onsite fossil fuel emissions from all new buildings, and by 2035, requiring zero onsite fossil fuel emissions from all large existing commercial buildings.

## GOALS:
1) Zero emisisons new construction by 2021
2) All large commerical buildings are zero emissions by 2035
3) All buildings zero emissions by 2040

3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is carbon-intensive, near-term emissions could shift upstream; however, the mechanism still targets GHG mitigation by removing combustion at point of use and enabling decarbonization as grids clean up.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that requires “zero onsite fossil fuel emissions” or bans onsite combustion shares the same primary mitigation pathway (direct emissions elimination from building operations).  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Any finer labeling (e.g., “building electrification,” “fuel switching,” “zero-emission buildings,” “retrofit vs new-build”) depends on policy-specific scope, compliance metrics, and grid context and is deferred to policy-level evidence.

mechanism_description: The policy sets goals to increase trips taken by low-carbon modes and to increase private vehicle electrification, which together reduce transportation greenhouse gas emissions.

primary_causal_pathway: Transport demand + vehicle technology shift → ↓ fossil fuel combustion (and upstream fuel emissions) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Travel behavior and transport system supply shift trips from single-occupant, internal-combustion vehicles to low-carbon modes (walking, cycling, public transit, shared mobility) and increase the share of electric private vehicles for remaining car trips.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced gasoline/diesel combustion lowers direct CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions; electrification shifts energy use to the power sector where emissions are typically lower per km and can decline further as the grid decarbonizes.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle transport-sector GHG emissions (primarily CO₂), lowering contribution to global warming.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions by decreasing fossil-fuel vehicle kilometers traveled and replacing remaining internal-combustion travel with electricity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The core pathway is reducing fossil-fuel use in transportation via (a) fewer/highly efficient car trips and (b) electrifying remaining private vehicle travel, yielding lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While there can be co-benefits (air quality, congestion, safety, energy efficiency), the dominant climate effect across policies using this mechanism is direct mitigation through emissions reductions, not primarily adaptation, resource efficiency as an end in itself, or nature-based carbon sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: If electricity is very carbon-intensive, EV benefits can be smaller, but the intended and typical dominant climate pathway remains transport GHG mitigation; mode shift still generally reduces emissions even under a high-carbon grid.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy combining sustainable mode shift and/or fleet electrification is fundamentally aimed at lowering transport combustion-related GHG emissions, so Mitigation remains the correct primary category for all such instances.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further categorization (e.g., “public transit expansion” vs “EV incentives” vs “pricing/management”) depends on the specific policy design and evidence at the individual policy level and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented mainly for congestion/air-quality with negligible net GHG change (e.g., EV uptake powered by high-emissions electricity and no VMT reduction), mitigation impact could be reduced, but the mechanism’s dominant climate-relevant intent and typical effect remains emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “By 2030”; “at least 80% of all trips”; “low-carbon modes”; “electrification of vehicles”; “at least 25% of all private vehicles registered”; “by 2040… 100%”
co_benefits: Air quality and public health benefits (reduced tailpipe pollution, more walking/cycling), congestion reduction, noise reduction, potential household fuel-cost savings.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although the goals imply potential future regulatory or investment actions, the provided excerpt itself contains targets only and does not specify enforcement mechanisms or programs; therefore classified as target-setting (enabling) rather than a mandate/standard.

section_header: EMISSIONS TARGETS AND CLIMATE GOALS / RESPONSIBLE PRODUCTION & CONSUMPTION GOALS
policy_statement: By 2030, reduce solid waste generation at least 15% below 2015 levels and reduce waste disposed by incineration or landfill at least 50% below 2015 levels.
verbatim_text:
TABLE 6: 2021 CLIMATE ACTION GOALS
ZEROWASTE  By 2030, a reduction in the generation of solid waste of at least 15% below 2015 levels and a reduction in the amount of solid waste disposed of by incineration or deposited in landfill of at least 50%below 2015 levels.

RESPONSIBLE PRODUCTION & CONSUMPTION
## GOALS:
By 2030:
1) Reduce solid waste generation 15% below 2015 levels
2) Reduce disposal to landfill 50% below 2015 levels
extraction_rationale: Quantified waste reduction and landfill disposal targets with deadline.
mechanism_description: The plan sets goals to reduce solid waste generation and reduce disposal to landfill or incineration, which lowers landfill-related greenhouse gas emissions, especially methane.
primary_causal_pathway: Waste generation/diversion changes → ↓ organic waste to landfill → ↓ anaerobic decomposition → ↓ CH₄ emissions → ↓ radiative forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in activity/system: Households, businesses, and city services prevent and/or divert food and other organics away from landfill (e.g., source reduction, redistribution, separate collection, composting/anaerobic digestion, circular business practices that reduce discards).  
(2) Mediating process: With less biodegradable carbon entering landfill conditions, there is less anaerobic microbial breakdown that produces methane over time (and potentially less landfill gas generation overall).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct GHG emissions, dominated by reduced CH₄ (and some CO₂) from waste disposal, yielding net mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided landfill methane emissions from reduced organic waste disposal.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main physical effect is reducing methane formation by keeping organics out of landfills, directly lowering GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: While “circular economy” and “zero-waste routes” can also improve material efficiency, the mechanism specified (and common across the examples) is explicitly oriented toward avoiding landfill CH₄—an emissions reduction outcome rather than primarily reducing resource/energy inputs for the same service.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some circular-economy policies may yield larger upstream manufacturing/energy savings than landfill CH₄ reductions depending on materials targeted; however, this canonical mechanism is defined as landfill methane avoidance via waste diversion/food waste reduction, so Mitigation remains primary for all policies that truly share this mechanism.  
4) Confirm applicability to ALL instances: Any policy fitting this mechanism necessarily reduces organic landfill disposal and therefore reduces landfill methane potential; that is inherently a mitigation pathway.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether a given policy’s secondary benefits are primarily “Resource Efficiency” (e.g., avoided production emissions) or include other co-benefits is policy- and implementation-dependent and should be assigned only with policy-level evidence.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “circular economy/zero-waste” initiatives may be dominated by upstream embodied-emissions reductions (resource efficiency) if they focus on non-organic materials; ensure the policy actually reduces organic landfill inputs when mapping to this mechanism. If a policy instead centers on landfill gas capture rather than diversion, it belongs to a different mechanism.
key_indicators: “By 2030”, “reduction… at least 15% below 2015 levels”, “reduction… disposed… by incineration or deposited in landfill… at least 50% below 2015 levels”, “2021 CLIMATE ACTION GOALS”, “ZEROWASTE”, “GOALS”
co_benefits: Reduced local air pollutants/toxics from incineration and landfill operations; reduced litter and related water quality impacts; extended landfill life/cost savings; resource conservation/circular economy benefits.
instance_edge_case_notes: Targets cover both landfill and incineration; the instrument is still best classified as target-setting because no binding mandate, pricing mechanism, or program/investment is specified in the excerpt.

policy_statement: To meet San Francisco's goal of zero emissions by 2040, all existing buildings must be renovated or replaced by 2040.
verbatim_text: As buildings are retrofitted and renovated, the stock of existing buildings decreases in this model. To meet San Francisco's goal of zero emissions by 2040, 100% of existing buildings must be renovated or replaced by 2040.
extraction_rationale: This is a quantified, deadline-based requirement explicitly linked to achieving the City's 2040 zero-emissions goal; it functions as a policy target/condition.
mechanism_description: Existing buildings are renovated or replaced over time, improving building performance and reducing energy use and associated emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Same building service → ↓ energy input → ↓ fuel combustion / ↓ grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Activity/system changed: Building design and operations are upgraded via retrofits (e.g., insulation, air sealing, efficient HVAC, heat recovery, LEDs, building automation, commissioning) and improved operational practices.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced heat losses/gains and improved equipment efficiency/control lowers final energy demand; this in turn reduces electricity generation and on-site fuel combustion needed to provide the same services.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from reduced energy use, with magnitude depending on the local electricity/heat carbon intensity and baseline performance.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions due to lower energy consumption for the same building services (heating, cooling, lighting, plug loads).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Retrofit-driven energy efficiency reduces energy demand for the same services, which primarily reduces combustion and grid generation emissions—i.e., a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While retrofits can also improve comfort, resilience (e.g., better thermal performance during heat/cold events), and reduce energy bills (resource efficiency framing), the largest climate-relevant effect across policies using this mechanism is reduced GHG emissions from lower energy consumption.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a retrofit increases electricity use (e.g., electrification with heat pumps) but reduces total emissions due to cleaner electricity, that is a different mechanism (fuel switching/electrification). If retrofits are primarily for passive survivability/adaptation without energy/emissions objectives, that would shift to Adaptation, but that is not what this mechanism encodes.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that fits “commercial/municipal building retrofit → energy use reduction” shares the same primary effect—lower energy demand leading to lower associated emissions—regardless of city or building type.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy should be tagged secondarily as Resource Efficiency (cost/energy savings emphasis) or Adaptation co-benefits (thermal resilience) depends on policy-level evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some retrofit packages include fuel switching (e.g., gas boiler to heat pump) or on-site renewables; those would introduce additional/alternative mechanisms. Rebound effects (increased comfort leading to higher use) can reduce net savings but typically do not overturn the dominant mitigation pathway.
key_indicators: “goal of zero emissions by 2040”; “100% of existing buildings must be renovated or replaced by 2040”
co_benefits: Improved indoor comfort/health; lower energy bills; potential resilience/safety improvements from renovations
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a regulatory mandate (because of “must”), but the core policy lever expressed here is a 2040 deadline/coverage target (100% of existing buildings), without specifying an enforceable performance metric (e.g., kBtu/sf or tCO₂e) or compliance mechanism.
